title: Optimizing the Answer, Hiding the Reason
subtitle: Outcome-only reinforcement learning erodes chain-of-thought monitorability against a known ground-truth cue
status: Smoke complete; real run designed, awaiting launch
summary: Chain-of-thought monitoring is one of the few interpretability tools that scales with capability, but it only works if a model's stated reasoning reflects the computation that produced its answer.

references.title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
references.annote: Origin of CoT prompting. The capability whose faithfulness this project interrogates.

references.title: Measuring Faithfulness in Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
references.annote: Defines faithfulness via interventions on the CoT (truncation, mistakes, paraphrase); larger models often less faithful. The measurement framing our reliance/articulation metrics build on.

references.title: Language Models Don't Always Say What They Think: Unfaithful Explanations in Chain-of-Thought Prompting
references.annote: Foundational unfaithful-CoT result. Source of the biasing-feature paradigm (a suggested answer the model follows without admitting it) that we adapt as the planted cue. Static prompted setting, no optimization pressure.

references.title: DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
references.annote: Introduces GRPO, the outcome-reward RL algorithm we train with.

references.title: Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior and the Risks of Promoting Obfuscation
references.annote: Direct optimization pressure on the CoT produces obfuscated reward hacking; argues for a monitorability tax. Closest mechanism precedent. Contrast: our reward is outcome-only with no direct CoT pressure, so any decay we observe is the weaker, more worrying emergent case.

references.title: Reasoning Models Don't Always Say What They Think
references.annote: Closest prior result. Outcome-based RL improves faithfulness then plateaus; reward-hacked hint usage is not verbalized. We must position against this directly: our contribution is a controlled decay curve vs RL steps against a known ground-truth cue with provably CoT-blind reward.

references.title: DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
references.annote: Pure-RL incentivization of reasoning. Motivates studying what outcome-only RL does to the resulting chains of thought.

references.title: Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity for AI Safety
references.annote: Multi-org position paper warning that scaled-up RL specifically threatens CoT monitorability. This project is a controlled empirical test of its central worry.

title: Error Awareness Is Format-Specific: An Arithmetic-Trained Wrongness Probe Does Not Transfer to Capital-City Facts
subtitle: A token-distribution transfer test on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct
status: Real run complete (RTX 5090). In-format AUC 0.968, cross-format AUC 0.649, capitals-internal AUC 0.990. Verdict: largely format-specific.
summary: A language model often assigns a different next-token distribution to a statement it has just completed depending on whether that statement is true or false, and a small classifier reading that distribution can recover whether the statement was correct. We ask a narrower question than prior work on whether such a signal exists.
compute: RTX 5090, torch cu130, transformers 4.57; model Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct

references.title: Discovering Latent Knowledge in Language Models Without Supervision
references.annote: CCS recovers a truth direction from contrastive activations without labels. Motivates the question of whether the truth/error representation is shared or domain-specific, which our transfer test probes behaviorally.

references.title: Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know
references.annote: Models can self-evaluate the probability their answers are correct (P(IK)), with calibration that varies by task. Frames the self-knowledge our probe reads out, and its task-dependence anticipates our format-specificity result.

references.title: The Internal State of an LLM Knows When It's Lying
references.annote: Shows a model's hidden activations linearly encode whether its own statement is true or false. The clearest precedent that an internal error signal exists; we ask whether a readout of that signal transports across statement formats.

references.title: The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets
references.annote: Finds linear true/false structure but reports that truth directions generalize unevenly across datasets/topics. Directly relevant: our finding that an arithmetic-trained readout barely transports to capitals is a behavioral echo of their representational topic-specificity.

title: Format-Specific Error Awareness Is Not Model-General: An Arithmetic-Trained Wrongness Probe Transfers Cleanly in Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct
status: Real run complete (RTX 5090). In-format AUC 0.9922, cross-format AUC 0.9817, gap 0.0105 (Qwen published gap: 0.3194). Verdict: the transfer collapse does not replicate; format-specificity is model-dependent.
summary: A recent transfer test on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct reported that a token-level error-awareness probe trained on arithmetic statements barely transfers to capital-city statements.
compute: RTX 5090, torch cu130, transformers 4.57; model meta-llama/Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct

references.title: Discovering Latent Knowledge in Language Models Without Supervision
references.annote: CCS recovers a truth direction from contrastive activations without labels. Motivates the question of whether the truth/error representation is shared or domain-specific, which our transfer test probes behaviorally.

references.title: Language Models (Mostly) Know What They Know
references.annote: Models can self-evaluate the probability their answers are correct (P(IK)), with calibration that varies by task. Frames the self-knowledge our probe reads out, and its task-dependence anticipates the format-specificity result we replicate here.

references.title: The Internal State of an LLM Knows When It's Lying
references.annote: Shows a model's hidden activations linearly encode whether its own statement is true or false. The clearest precedent that an internal error signal exists; we ask whether a readout of that signal transports across statement formats and across model families.

references.title: The Geometry of Truth: Emergent Linear Structure in Large Language Model Representations of True/False Datasets
references.annote: Finds linear true/false structure but reports that truth directions generalize unevenly across datasets/topics. Directly relevant: format-specificity of a token-level readout is the behavioral echo of their representational topic-specificity, and we test whether that echo repeats in a second model family.

references.title: The Llama 3 Herd of Models
references.annote: Describes the Llama 3.1 family including the 8B Instruct model we probe. Cited as the source and documentation of the second model under test; its training pipeline differs from Qwen's, which is what makes the replication informative.

references.title: Qwen2.5 Technical Report
references.annote: Describes the Qwen2.5 family including the 7B Instruct model of the original study. Cited as the documentation of the model whose published numbers we compare against.

title: Format-Specificity of Error Awareness Is Model-Dependent
status: Workshop paper synthesizing five verified runs. Forward: Mistral patterns with Llama, Qwen is the three-family outlier. Reverse: Qwen's collapse is bidirectional; Llama inverts below chance (0.432). Hard target: internal controls de-ceilinged; Llama transfers at ceiling, Qwen falls below chance (0.337). Portability belongs to the (model, training format, target task) triple.
summary: Token-level error-awareness probes read a model's next-token distribution at the moment it would commit to a statement and ask whether the model knows the statement is wrong.
compute: RTX 5090, torch cu130, transformers 4.57; models Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct, Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct-v0.3

references.title: Discovering Latent Knowledge in Language Models Without Supervision
references.annote: CCS recovers a truth direction from contrastive activations without labels. Motivates the question of whether the truth/error representation is shared or domain-specific, which our transfer test probes behaviorally.

references.title: Mistral 7B
references.annote: Describes the Mistral 7B family; v0.3 is the third model family added by the workshop paper's generalization run, cited as the documentation of that model.

references.annote: Finds linear true/false structure but reports that truth directions generalize unevenly across datasets/topics. Directly relevant: format-specificity of a token-level readout is the behavioral echo of their representational topic-specificity, and we test whether that echo repeats in a second model family.

references.annote: Describes the Llama 3.1 family including the 8B Instruct model we probe. Cited as the source and documentation of the second model under test; its training pipeline differs from Qwen's, which is what makes the replication informative.

references.annote: Describes the Qwen2.5 family including the 7B Instruct model of the original study. Cited as the documentation of the model whose published numbers we compare against.

title: Most of the Gain Was Already There
subtitle: Three elicitation tests on a small KL-anchored GRPO run: what reweighting explains, and the residue it does not
status: Run complete - training, eval grid, analysis, verify gate, and two reviewer-panel rounds all finished; published after human approval.
summary: GRPO on GSM8K lifts a small model's held-out pass@1 by 5.8 points, and the paired per-problem confidence interval on that gain (0.0466-0.0695) excludes zero.

references.title: Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code
references.annote: Source of the unbiased pass@k estimator implemented in analysis/passk.py

references.title: Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems
references.annote: GSM8K, the training task and primary eval slice

references.title: Measuring Mathematical Problem Solving With the MATH Dataset
references.annote: MATH, source of the harder eval slice (via MATH-500) and its normalization-based grading

references.title: LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models
references.annote: LoRA adapters, the parameterization of the GRPO policy update

references.title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain of Thought Reasoning in Language Models
references.annote: The majority-vote (self-consistency) selector used by the elicit_bon arm

references.title: Let's Verify Step by Step
references.annote: Source of the 500-problem representative MATH test subset (MATH-500) our harder slice draws from; verified against the arXiv abstract page 2026-06-11

references.title: Stress-Testing Capability Elicitation With Password-Locked Models
DeepSeekMath: Pushing the Limits of Mathematical Reasoning in Open Language Models
references.annote: Introduces GRPO, the training algorithm

references.title: AI Sandbagging: Language Models can Strategically Underperform on Evaluations
Qwen2.5 Technical Report
references.annote: The model family; we train Qwen2.5-1.5B-Instruct

references.title: ProRL: Prolonged Reinforcement Learning Expands Reasoning Boundaries in Large Language Models
references.annote: 2k+ RL steps, ~16k H100 GPU-hours; released Nemotron-Research-Reasoning-Qwen-1.5B

references.title: Reinforcement Learning Finetunes Small Subnetworks in Large Language Models
references.annote: RL updates 5-30 percent of parameters across 7 algorithms and 10 models

references.title: RAST: Reasoning Activation in LLMs via Small-model Transfer
references.annote: RL-induced probability shifts are approximately model-size invariant and transferable

references.title: Spurious Rewards: Rethinking Training Signals in RLVR
references.annote: Random rewards recover 21.4 of 29.1 points on MATH-500 for Qwen2.5-Math-7B; effect is model-family dependent

references.title: RL Grokking Recipe: How Does RL Unlock and Transfer New Algorithms in LLMs?
references.annote: DELTA-Code benchmark; pass@K=0 problem families become solvable via grokking phase transition

references.title: Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning in Large Language Models with One Training Example
references.annote: 1-shot RLVR lifts Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B from 36.0 to 73.6 on MATH500

references.title: The Invisible Leash: Why RLVR May or May Not Escape Its Origin
references.annote: Support-constraint analysis; answer-level entropy declines even as token-level entropy rises

references.title: Depth-Breadth Synergy in RLVR: Unlocking LLM Reasoning Gains with Adaptive Exploration
references.annote: DARS: difficulty-adaptive rollout sampling; GRPO underweights hard problems

references.title: The Debate on RLVR Reasoning Capability Boundary: Shrinkage, Expansion, or Both? A Two-Stage Dynamic View
references.annote: Early exploitation shrinks the boundary, prolonged exploration expands it

references.title: Does Reinforcement Learning Really Incentivize Reasoning Capacity in LLMs Beyond the Base Model?
references.annote: The pass@k crossover / "Limit of RLVR" paper

references.title: New Skills or Sharper Primitives? A Probabilistic Perspective on the Emergence of Reasoning in RLVR
references.annote: Step-level probability sharpening compounds into solving previously unsolvable composite tasks
